utterances: But demand from the ethanol industry, livestock feed and export market continues to out-strip yield projections, says Iowa State University economist Chad Hart, and that sent grain prices soaring.
When you look, last summer, we had corn prices, in some cases, down around $3.50 a bushel.
Now, we're up in the 6 to $7 range, so we've seen nearly a doubling of prices, if you will, over that past 15 months.
And in the last few months, another player has taken a bite out of the US corn market.

China's been a very early buyer of the corn crop we're growing now and so they've already, if you will, put in some advance purchases and that's been something that has got the market a little worked up right now.
Now, high grain prices are usually good news for farmers.
They helped Vernon Flinn buy a new tractor, but today's even higher prices mean even higher stakes and Flinn says selling in this volatile market comes with risks.
But then, when the disappointments inevitably came along, people took them extremely personally.
I think we, perhaps, expected more of her, and we expect more of a woman that we do have a man in general.
And somehow, the disappointments hurt more keenly.
Susan Morrison, editor of the new book ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary."" Again, if you'd like to join us, 800-989-8255.
E-mail talk@npr.org.
And let's start out with Amy(ph).
Amy calling us from Myrtle Beach in South Carolina.
Hi.
How are you doing today?
Very well thank you.
Good.
I was calling because I just had a comment about Hillary Clinton.
I'm compelled to vote for her, as a woman voter, I want to have a female president.
But I have reservations about voting for Hillary because sometimes in the public eye, she comes up as so hard and so cold.
And I feel conflicted about that because sometimes they feel like the media jumps on her when she shows emotion.
Yes indeed.
For example, a couple of weeks ago just before the New Hampshire primary, there was a that now-famous emotional moment.
Let's listen.
(Soundbite of past interview)
It's not easy.
It's not easy.
And I couldn't do it if I just didn't, you know, passionately believe it was the right thing to do.
You know, I have so many opportunities from this country.
I just don't want to see us fall backwards, you know.
So…
(Soundbite of applause)
And Amy, I wonder if you saw or heard that before and how you responded to it?
I did.
And actually for me, that's more of the Hillary Clinton that I would like to see because it shows so much her human side and so many times, I feel like she's almost mechanical.
And I have to say that, Susan Morrison, a lot of your writers addressed that very question, the fear that she's cold.
Yes.
Well, it - a lot of the writers, Deborah Tannen in particular, lists the adjectives that are routinely used to describe Hillary: cold, calculating, aloof.
And as Deborah points out in her essay, would anybody ever call Rudy Giuliani cold?
Would anyone think to call John McCain calculating?
These just aren't really adjectives that you routinely hear applied to men in a pejorative way.
And it's sort of underscores how we do have different expectations from what we want a woman to be.
One of the things that I think floats to the top when you have read all 30 of these essays is that there still seems to be quite a bit of national confusion about what we expect from a woman leader.
One of the things that I thought that was so interesting about New Hampshire is that after Iowa, basically, all the media and all the pundits has just said, oop(ph), Hillary sunk, this is it.
And even after the so-called crying moment, there was almost an instant consensus that, oh my God, she's finished.
This is terrible.
When…
Yeah.
You can't cry in a campaign.
…in fact - yeah, everyone kept saying, it was her Muskie moment and that it was over.
But in fact, what happened is that voters, particularly women, really rallied around her and, you know, she won the primary with a huge boost from women.
I think that women and men like to see that she's human.
But it is interesting that it's just - it is a criticism that we rarely make of male candidates.
We rarely say, oh, I wish we saw them to be warmer and fuzzier, but somehow because she's a woman, we just - we want to see that.
The Muskie reference to former Senator Ed Muskie, a presidential contender who may have or did - may or may not have cried and lost apparently the nomination or at least that's the perception.
Because of that, she was not referring to a freshwater fish.
In any case, Amy, do you plan to vote for Senator Clinton?
I am so undecided.
I'm very, very torn but I've been enjoying listening to you all of the NPR coverage on the elections.
So I don't know if I'm going to make up my mind before I actually headed to the voting booth.
Well, have a good time.
Thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.
Bye-bye.
Here to share her views on Hillary is Dahlia Lithwick, the senior legal correspondent and senior editor at the online magazine Slate.com, also legal correspondent for NPR's DAY TO DAY.
She joins us from member station WCVE in Richmond, Virginia.
And it's nice to talk with you again.
Hi, Neal.
And I'm just curious about the title of your essay.
Can You Forgive Her?"" What are you talking about?
Well, I should bracket what I'm about to say by saying that that probably Susan should speak to the titles because I don't think I'm in charge of that.
So if I want to - so Susan, should I spike it to you right now?
Well, I tell you very quickly.
When I was putting the final touches on this collection, I made an effort to let the titles - made sure that the titles differentiated each essay from the rest as much as possible.

And I thought that the gist of Dahlia's was, you know, there are all of these things that bug us about Hillary, let's figure out if they make sense of not, can we forgive her for what - now we should bounce it back to Dahlia so she can sum it for us (unintelligible).
Okay, okay.
Okay.
This is that female spirit of collaboration we're talking about, Neal.
And blaming the editor for writing the headline.
Elizabeth, I wonder how messages like that change or don't change your opinion of Senator Clinton.
But in any case, unpopularity and lack of time, those are the two things that we have to discuss right now.
Rona, thanks very much for the phone call.
Thank you.
No.
I just didn't want to massively misrepresent.
I mean, I think what I was struggling with - and I think Susan sort of laid it out - is this sort of head-heart problem that I have and then a lot of women have, you know.
I desperately want to be more excited about this candidate.
You know, I want my children to have had a woman president and I - her resume, you know, is everything and more that one could ask for and yet, I just can't warm to her.

And, you know, as I sort of tried to unpack what it is that I can't forgive in Hillary it's a lot of this very, very gendery, weird, Freudy(ph) and psychosocial stuff that I think women can't forgive in themselves.
And so, you know, a lot of the things that I laid out on my chapter are, you know, can we not forgive her because she reminds us of our mothers?
Can we not forgive her because, you know, she stood by Bill and looked like a victim after Lewinskygate?

Can we not forgive her because she reminds us of the sort of mommy wars that, you know, the wars that are going on where women in this country are setting each other's hair on fire over this question of going to work or staying at home.
And I think as a template for all of that anxiety that we have about ourselves - are we good enough?
Are we faking it in a man's world?
You know, I described running around with a Cheerio in my hair, which is in fact true, you know, trying to pretend I'm a real serious journalist - we don't see any of that messiness in her.
And I think sort of the arch of my chapter was meant to be women like the messiness.
I think we want to know.
It's not just that moment in New Hampshire where she teared up and it let us know she was authentic.
We like the sort of Lifetime Television for women story about, you know, I was wrong, I scrabbled back, here I am, I'm at the top of the heap.
And we really haven't seen that messiness in Hillary.
And that's one of the things I think we can't forgive her.
Well, some women - you write in your essay, I've yet to encounter a woman who adores candidate Clinton, who wants to staff phone banks and wear T-shirts for her.
Tepid endorsements, yes.
Moderate heebie-jeebies, yes.
But passion, devotion, strong personal identification, never.
And yet there are plenty of women in her headquarters wearing those t-shirts and just as passionate, but maybe not the women you're talking to.

Well, I think that - and, you know, remembering that I wrote that, you know, my sort of essay several months ago, so I think it was probably before this campaign had really, really started to heat up.

But, yeah, you know, my experiences with young mothers, young professional women, I think there's an enormous disjunction between, you know, one generation of feminists and the next on Hillary Clinton.
And I think that young women, you know, who have toddlers like I do, don't see ourselves in her quite as much as the generation of feminists before us.

I think that that incredibly strong essay that Gloria Steinem wrote a few weeks ago in The New York Times, essentially sort of admonishing women to get behind Hillary, just didn't resonate with women who are, you know, of the younger sort of, let's say, the next wave of feminists.
And Susan Morrison, let me put this to you.
You do hear from some African-Americans that not to vote for Barack Obama, well, that's meaning you're a traitor to your race.
Do you hear from anybody not to vote for Hillary Clinton is being a traitor to your sex?

Well, actually, one thing that I feel like we're hearing a little bit more of as the campaign goes on is - well, and (unintelligible) says, she put it this way, she said, she's never been a big Hillary fan but she says, if people keep saying these horrible, insulting, sexist things about Hillary Clinton, I may just have to vote for her.

And I think that as the campaign goes on and Hillary absorbs more and more flak and sexism and, you know, negative comments, I think that there is a kind of inexorable sense of women rallying around her and women who were never hugely enthusiastic about her.
And I think that might go on and on.
But to refer back to the Gloria Steinem op-ed piece that Dahlia just mentioned, I actually found that quite powerful.

I mean, I am maybe half a generation or maybe a whole generation older than Dahlia, but Steinem made the point that she said, listen, African-American men had the vote 50 years before women had the vote in this country.
It's just crazy to act as if women have already completely arrived.
I mean, if that's what it is where have been all the other, you know, near-miss female presidential candidates?
There's just no denying that it will be huge thing for us to have either a woman president or an African-American president.
And it's - I don't think it's terribly productive to debate over which is the cooler thing.
I mean, both of them are amazing.
And the fact that we have two candidates, these two breakthroughs, you know, potentially happening, is just great.
I mean, it's great for our country and it's going to be a fascinating election to watch.
